bibl.author: Lovell, George W.
bibl.title: The Wife's Secret
An Original Play in five acts
Lacy's Acting Edition, volume 82, No. 1216

event.desc: Premiered at Theatre Royal, Haymarket 7 Jan. 1848; Nicoll date 1848-01-17

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible

titlePage.byline: BYGEORGE W. LOVELL,AUTHOR OF "The Provost of Bruges;" "Love's Sacrifice;" Look before you Leap;" "The Trial of Love;" "The Trustee," a Novel; &c.
titlePage.titlePart: [With alterations and additions by the Author.]

div.p: TO MRS. CHARLES KEAN, AND TO THE MEMORY OF HER LATE LAMENTED HUSBAND, This Play,WHICH WAS SO DEEPLY INDEBTED TO THEIR GENIUS,IS INSCRIBED WITH SINCERE REGARD BY THE AUTHOR.

performance.p: Produced at the Theatre Royal Haymarket, on Monday, the 17th of January, 1848.

castList.head: The Wife's Secret
Characters.
castList.note: Original Cast at the Haymarket Theatre.

set.p: SCENE.-Near the Sea Coast, in Dorsetshire. Time, 1655.

list.label: ACT I.
list.item: Sir WALTER AMYOTT'S HOUSE ON THE COAST OF DORSETSHIRE. THE BOWER CHAMBER.
list.label: ACT II.
list.item: Terrace before the house. The bower chamber. The shrubbery.
list.label: ACT III.
list.item: Sir WALTER'S STUDY. EXTERIOR OF THE BOWER CHAMBER.
list.label: ACT IV.
ACT V.

front.note: The following Play was purchased by the late Mr. Charles Kean, and sincehis lamented death, has been most kindly restored by Mrs. Kean to theAuthor, who has now revised it throughout, with numerous alterations of the original, and additions of new matter.

div.head: SCENE FIRST.– Court of Sir Walter Amyott's House.
div.stage: A loud knocking heard, L. Enter 1st Servant, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: 1st Servant.
sp.l: Yes, it's a knocking–and Sir Walter absent–
And no one but my lady left at home,
And us poor servants.
Yes, it is a knocking.
What can it be? It's William's business
To answer that front gate. (knocking louder) Most certainly
It is a knocking. Should it now be thieves,
Or cavalier malignants! Well, I'm glad
It's not my duty.
There can't be a doubt
That it's a knocking; but I'm not the man
To take another's office.

sp.speaker: Courier.
sp.l: Ho! Hilloa!
Hilloa there! Ho! Within the house! (knocking) Why, knaves!
'Tis easy seen the master is from home.
Within there! Ho!

div.stage: Enter 2nd and 3rd Servants, R. 1 E., they cross and exeunt, L. 1 E., and return with Courier.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: I wonder if they are armed.
Verily, honest man, thou mak'st a noise–
What, Robert? Welcome back to Dorsetshire.
You shameless knaves(to Servants)to keep good Robert waiting!

sp.speaker: 3rd Serv.
sp.l: Why, boy, thou'lt bring rare news, I warrant now.

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: The best of news.
Sir Walter's coming home; and sends me first
With this to greet his lady.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Verily
'Twill cheer her soul, I doubt not. She hath pined
Most fondly in his absence–like as one
Whose heart clings to its creature idol.

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: Quick then,
And bear that comfort to her.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Honest lady!
Ay, as thou say'st, 'twill comfort her. Here, William,
sp.stage: (giving the letter to Second Servant, L.)
sp.l: It is thine office; bear this to my lady,
And look thou loiter not. Her spirit hungers
For tidings of my lord. There's food for her. –
And so – the news, good lad?

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: You've heard, belike,
Cromwell is now Protector, and His Highness –

sp.speaker: 2nd Serv.
sp.l: Aye marry! the good brewer's ale grows strong!

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: What, thou'rt not gone! I'll have thee whipt, thou knave!
sp.stage: (Second Servant crosses slowly behind to R.)
sp.l: And so His Highness–well, good lad–His Highness–

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: Aye, the malignant Royalists broke out
So mad upon this title, that they rose
And entered Salisbury.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Good lack! good lack!
The cut-throat men of war! But Salisbury,
That's far from here– there is no danger?

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: Danger!
A single troop of horse dispersed them all.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Aha! The knaves! I wish some few of them
Would fly this way. In troth my lord should find
We're men of valour here. 'Twould well content me
To make a prisoner.

sp.speaker: 3rd Serv.
sp.l: And me.

sp.speaker: 2nd Serv.
sp.l: And me.

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: Why look, that varlet holds the letter still.

sp.speaker: 2nd Serv.
sp.l: Francis, fly with it; art thou not ashamed
To keep my lady waiting?

sp.speaker: 3rd Serv.
sp.l: Nay, but Robert–
For these malignants?

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: To prevent more mischief
Cromwell sends men of trust to every province.
Sir Walter comes commissioned here–

sp.speaker: 3rd Serv.
sp.l: Oh my brave master!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Sir Walter?

div.stage: (The Servants all exhibit boisterous joy, turning up the Stage as the Page enters, R. 1 E.)

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Why what hubbub's here?
Knocking and shouting, and all heads together?
My lady sends to know of this disturbance.
What's this– a letter?
William should have took it.

sp.speaker: 3rd Serv.
sp.l: No, it was given Peter.

sp.speaker: 2nd Serv.
sp.l: Robert brought it

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: My life now; but if swearing were permitted
You'd make me swear! A letter for my lady,
And none of all these idle knaves will stir
To bear it to her! Get you gone, you varlets!
Off! or I'll have the dog-whips out! (The Servants cross behind and slink off, R. 1 E.) Stay, fellow!
A letter. (turning it over) Hem! And from my lord, I think?

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: It is so, sir.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: My lady will be wild
Till she receive it! Do you guess its tidings?
sp.stage: (turning it over, and trying to look into it)

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: To tell, as I believe, Sir Walter's coming.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Ha! coming home? I' faith I'm glad to hear it.
We have been sadly moped. I've been, myself,
The only man of any note in the house
These six months past: now we shall have companions.
I think they say my lord is much renowned?

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: So much so, that he comes in full command
Over this district–above all appeal,
Save to the Lord Protector's self.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Nay, faith then
Such trust is very creditable to him!
You come, I think, from London? Met you any
Who asked for me? for when I last was there
I left some bright eyes wet. I should be sad
To learn the foolish things had pined too deeply.

div.stage: Enter Maud, L., Courier takes off his hat.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: How now, sir Page! They say there's tidings come
Of my good lord. My lady waits impatient,
And sends me down to ask of your delay.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: I did but pause, sweet Maud, to rate this fellow
For loitering on the road.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: A letter, too!
Give it to me I'll bear it her myself.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Thank you; I know a page's duty better.
I'll spare your pains, and win myself the largess
For the good news. Fellow!(to Courier) go to the buttery
And bid the cellarer fill thee with ale.

sp.speaker: Cour.
sp.l: I humbly thank your Pageship; and I'll go
Drink to your better beard.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Well, this is cool!
These imps of Pages are the very devil–
Oh, mercy on me! what a naughty word!
It's well none heard me. But 'tis really hard
To stroke oneself down to the sleek demureness
Of these smooth-spoken times. Now here comes one
Would read a homily ag ood hour long
On my enormity; yet, I'll be sworn
The knave would cheat or lie–backbite or slander,
Without a blush – but mum! Suddenly changing her manner as Jabez Sneed enters, R. 1 E. So, master steward,
They say the man, called carnally our master,
Returneth home.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: They speak without a lie then–
A most rare grace.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: 'Twill be a joyous day
For my dear lady.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Will it?

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Do you doubt it?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: No. Walter Amyott is rich and young:
Such ever find a welcome.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Sir! my master–
(And bear in mind he is your master too) –
Is noble, generous, faithful, honest, brave!
Some servants might well take him for their pattern.
And, for my lady's love –the doting fondness
Of their first courtship was but cold and weak
Gauged by the wife's devotion.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: They've been married
Almost a twelvemonth!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Well–and if they have, sir?
Twelve ages would not change a love like theirs.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Hem!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Jabez Sneed, you do not love my lady.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I'm not her husband–wherefore should I love her?
Because she came a wolf among our lambs?
Bred of a family of hot malignants–
Daughter of that Lord Arden who at Naseby
Fell fighting by the man Charles Stuart's side;
Sister of that Lord Arden, his successor,
Who, after Worcester's crowning mercy, fled
To pitch his tent among his country's foes,
And plot against the rulers of his people?
I tell thee there be those would hold the deed.
A pious one to smite, with the sword's edge,
The Amalekitish woman!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: She's no woman,
Thou saucy knave! She is a noble lady!
And for thy hate of her, thou cankerous varlet,
It is not that she's daughter to a lord,
Nor sister to a banished nobleman;
But that she's wife to thy too trusting master,
And more than doubts thy long accustomed plunder!
That she knows figures–looks to thy accounts–
Balances incomings and outgoings,
And keeps thee straight in all. Why thou'rt grown thinner
By half thy substance since thou canst not thieve!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Verily, Maud, the world is strong in thee.
Thou talk'st of carnal things.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I talk of thee,
A very carnal thing! My lady too
Talks of thee, Jabez Sneed, and will talk more
When my good lord is come. Aha! sir knave,
She has some rare discoveries for his ear
Of thy past doings, and she'll make thee known
For what thou art.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Thou'rt somewhat out of breath–
Better sit down and rest.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I've breath enough
To tell my lady what thou say'st of her;
Ay, and I'll do it too! A woman quotha!
Out on the scurvy knave! My lord shall know
He's married to a woman when he comes!
Marry, I marvel what he'll say to that.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Sweet mistress Maud; 'tis sometimes well to cross thee,
For thou speak'st plainly then. I am suspected!
'Tis well I know it. And the pretty doubts
Are to be prattled in Sir Walter's ear.
This daughter of the land –this painted doll–
This plaything of a wife would overturn
My forty years of service! I will gird
The loins of my discretion, and do battle
To hold my credit and to weaken hers.
'Twere best she walk advisedly, or I
May trip her. Then, my dainty Lady Eveline,
Look to thyself! There is an eye upon thee
May find some specks of black in all thy snow.

div.head: SCENE SECOND. –T
div.stage: he Bower Chamber in Sir Walter Amyott's House. Night. Door, L. 2 E.; carpet down.
Lady Eveline discovered, seated, with a letter in her hand, L. C.

sp.speaker: Lady Eveline.
sp.l: Amyott returning! Walter coming home !
Thou dear conveyance of precious news,
A blessing on thine every character!
How kind–how tenderly he writes! What love!
Oh, I am undeserving of such bliss!
What shall I do to merit it? And thou,
Dear paper, that hast sped so fast before,
Impatient to anticipate my joy,
What largess shall I give thee for thy pains?
He will not grudge this kiss–'tis given to him,
For there his hand has rested. He has touched thee–
His finger traced these lines. I see his eye
That bent above them, tracking the swift pen,
And with its look of love, as with a seal,
Impressing each fond syllable with truth.
Dear Walter! Dearest Husband!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Please, my lady,
Or I, or Jabez Sneed, must quit this house!
The ill-conditioned varlet–

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Peace, good Maud!
There's nothing ill-conditioned now. All's fair,
Joyous and good! Sir Walter's coming home!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: And if you do not make his earliest act
To turn his knavish steward out of doors,
You merit no good service. By my life,
I know not what the pestilent rogues would have.
I've clipt my dress down to their primmest cut,
Foregone my ribbons and my naughty words,
Turned up my eyes, and spoken through my nose–

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Poor Maud! poor Maud!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Ay, you may laugh, my lady;
Pretty demure behaviours cost you nothing,
For you were born an angel; I have had
To make myself one–no small task I promise!
And what's our recompense? Now I'll just tell you
What Jabez said of you.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Nay, do not, Maud;
Say what he may he cannot anger me.
I am vexation-proof with happiness!
Walter is coming home! (three distinct taps are heard at the window, c.) Hush! What was that?

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: It sounded like a tapping at the casement!
Mercy! why there's a man! I saw his head!
Shall I call "fire?"

sp.speaker: Lord Arden.
sp.l: Hist, Eveline!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: My name !
It must be Walter!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Climbing like a thief
To that back window?

sp.speaker: Lord Arden.
sp.l: Eveline! quick, quick!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: That voice! Impossible! Fly, Maud–the door!
Secure the door!
sp.stage: (Maud hastily bolts door, L. 2 E., then stands up at back, L.–Lady Eveline rushes to the window and opens it–Lord Arden springs into the room)

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Philip! can this be real?

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Exceedingly substantial. (embraces her) Feel it.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: But how here?
I thought you safe in France.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: So I was, once;
And would I were again! In short, sweet sister,
Your rascal Noll, assuming royal state,
Was too much for my bile. I crept to England,
Joined a few honest fellows in the West,
And seized on Salisbury–judges and all!
But none supported us. We got well thrashed;
Penruddock, Groves, and half a score beside,
Were taken prisoners, and lost their heads–
While I, less known, or better horsed, escaped,
And made for Dorsetshire, to beg a shelter
Till I can find a ship for France again,

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh, Philip!–and thy life's in danger?

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Rather!
A goose's neck, if caught at Michaelmas,
Would be about as safe! I tell thee, too, girl,
There is some peril in concealing me;
But I mistake my sister Eveline
If she will heed for that.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Though 'twere my life
That should be given in exchange for thine,
Thou know'st I would not grudge it. What my means,
My credit, influence–but why say mine?
My husband's heart is one with mine in all things.
He too–

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Stay, Eveline! I have forgiven
Your marriage with this Roundhead–for, in truth,
All tongues report him as an honest man,
Worthy a better party; but I swear
Ere I would owe him thanks for any service,
Even the lightest–

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Philip, be not rash–
You do not know him.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Nor intend to know.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: He is generous.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: I will not tax his bounty.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: This is unjust! You misconstrue my words.
He has a heart that beats for honour only–
A chivalrous high spirit that would let
A world float by, rather than stoop by baseness
To snatch advantage of a matchless courage,
All good men's idol, yet withal so humble,
He is the only one who knows it not;
A heart as pure and warm as infancy,
And where it loves, unbounded in devotion.
Each hour expects him here. He comes with power
To make his will effective; and that will,
That power, shall be for your protection.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Sister,
Hear what I say, and do not after think
To change my resolution. When I first
Learned you had wedded Walter Amyott,
My rage, that any prick-eared cur should dare
To marry with my sister, knew no bounds.
I heaped upon him insults–public ones–
Challenged him–called his temper cowardice,
That would not meet me with as blind a hate–
Outraged him as I thought no man could bear,
Though, for your sake, he did endure it still–
And having done this, think you I would now
Crouch down and ask for service from him? Bid him,
Who comes here in authority and trust,
Give shelter to his master's enemy?
Raise war between his pity and his duty?
Assault his conscience! For these gentlemen,
You know, have very tender consciences.
Nay, do not interrupt me, Eveline.
Promise me–swear to me, no word, no hint
Shall ever reach to Walter Amyott's ear
I am so much as debtor to his walls
For shelter from the sky! Promise me this,
Or on the instant I'll resume my flight,
And dare all consequence.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Well, well, I promise.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Swear it! (pause) Swear it!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I do!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Enough––I'm satisfied!
Remember, Eveline, I have thine oath !
Forgive me, if stern dangers make me harsh.
Thou know'st 'tis not my nature; but these times,
Change better men than I. Now, I but ask
A shelter of my sister–no great boon,
And one she'll not deny. For honest Maud here,
Whom I hold sworn as deeply as yourself,
I will not grudge her knowledge of my secret:
And, though she tries to look as sanctified
As any of their crew, there still peeps out
A merry little devil from her eye,
That says, all's right within.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I' faith, my lord–
And that's a bigger oath than I've had courage
To use this twelvemonth past–you need not fear
I shall turn traitor; ere they get a word
Out of my lips, the rogues shall take my tongue;
And that, or I mistake, would be a gift
They would be little grateful for..

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Good wench,
I'll seal the bargain with thee.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: La! my lord!
Just the same merry gentleman as ever.
sp.stage: (a distant trumpet and shouts heard, L.)

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Hark, he is come! My husband is arrived!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Pest on him! Can't he give me breathing time?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh, short of sight! How little did I guess
That I could tremble at my Walter's coming!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Better go out to meet him.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: But my brother–

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Ay, there's the thing. Where shall we stow his lordship?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Hush! There are voices in the hall–a footstep–
I know it–it is his–Walter! my husband!
Oh, Philip, let me make you friends at once,
And be all happiness!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Another word,
And I am gone for ever.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Cruel!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Quick!
I hear him at the door. Stay–here–the closet!
For heaven's sake keep still though, or you'll make
Some mischief.
sp.stage: (Lady Eveline forces Lord Arden into the closet –Maud runs and unfastens the door, and having done so, stands demurely, with her hands before her, up stage, L.)

div.stage: Enter Sir Walter, door, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: Eveline! My wife!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh, Walter!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: My own, my precious one!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: And thou art safe,
And well?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: My long lost, my recovered treasure!
I did not think time had the power to spin
Such weary length from so few numbered weeks
As those since I have parted from thee. Truly
'Tis but ill husbandry of life, to love;
Thriftlessly storing in one fragile vessel
The heart's whole wealth, which lost, we are all bankrupt.
I sometimes fear I doat too fondly on thee!
For every yearning thought of hope, joy, fame,
Country and freedom, all like restless birds
In circles flutter till they light on thee! –
The soldier's victory, the patriot's triumph,
Are still imperfect until thou shalt share them.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: And dost thou grudge to love, then? Once thou saidst
That was the guiding lamp that led thee on
To honourable deeds–to fame–to glory.
Are these no part of life?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ay, the rough waves
On which the mariner is tossed, through which he labours,
Because they wash the shore where stands his home.
In the hot race the goal fills all the eye;
The rest is weary labour. So to me
Fame, honour, glory, are but toilsome ways,
Through which I seek a path to lead me home
More worthy of thy smile! Nay, that's a tear.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: The overflowing of a heart filled up
Too full with happiness.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Yet, now I look,
There is a shade of care upon thy brow–
A paleness on thy cheek.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: And is it nothing
To know embarked on such a stormy sea
My all of wealth! Oh, Walter! will the jar
Of these discordant times never subside
To harmony again? Each rumour thrills me
With terrors for the fate of some I love;
Close linked with both divisions! There has been
Another rising?.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ay, at Salisbury–
A mad-brained unsupported foolery,
Not worth a thought; save for the wretched men
Who led it on.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: And they?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Have paid the forfeit.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: What all?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: As I have heard.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Did none escape?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: None that are known.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Thank heaven!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Eveline!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: What did I say? You must not heed my words,
Where sometimes distant thoughts go linked together.
Yet is it not a cause for joy that none
Are doomed to rove the land like hunted beasts,
With bloodhounds on their track–in restless flight,
Chased day by day, a price upon their heads;
Their common kind, with cold and hunger linked
To quench the struggling spark of hope within;
Till worn, exhausted, they sink down to earth,
And the warm blood that filled a noble heart,
Gluts the pursuer's fangs. It might have been
That even thou, in thy stern duty's course,
Cam'st here on such an errand.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: My commission
Is of a milder tenour–to forestall,
Not punish wrong.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Thou art not angry with me?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Angry!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Or wilt forgive–remembering I've a brother
Has been a fugitive–may be again.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Lord Arden is in France; and I would trust
Too wise to tempt again a hopeless fortune.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: And even if he should, and thou should'st meet him,
I know thou would'st not see in him a foe,
But only Eveline's brother?

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: Eveline,
When I took arms there was no selfish thought
In my heart's purpose–no exciting dream
Of interest or glory urged me on.
I rose in answer to the holiest call
That ever sounded on a freeman's ear.
My faith–my country asked their children's aid;–
Such as I could I gave. I would not boast,
But thou hast somewhat known that private wrong
Falls dully on my nature. To the foe
Who slanders me–abuses my forbearance,
And undermines the fabric of my peace,
I still can give–have given the hand of pardon;
But should one born and nursed my country's child
Still plot against her freedom–should he, now
That she has won her liberty, still seek
To fasten chains upon her sacred limbs,
And pour in poison on her yet green wounds,
Then would I reckon such my deadliest foe!
Would own no tie of kin–no link of love;–
For such

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Enough, enough! (Sir Walter turns up, L. aside) Philip was right,
'Twere death to trust my brother's secret with him!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: If now Lord Arden hears him there'll be murder!
I think my lady, sir, looks very pale–
This room oppresses her. It's monstrous close,
You would do well to take her in the air.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Oppresses her! the night is sharp and cold.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Yes, very true; but yet this nasty room–
She's always ill in it, arn't you, my lady?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I should indeed be easier elsewhere.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I tell you, sir, she can't abide this room.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Not this–her Bower Chamber! I had thought it
Was ever in her eyes, of all the house,
The favourite.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Oh yes, it used to be:
But while you were away, she would set here
Such weary hours watching for tidings from you,
We nicknamed it the room of tears and sighs!
A thousand times she has said, when you came home,
If come you should (and then she wept again),
She'd never see it more, but lock it up,
With all its memories of care inside,
And throw away the key. (aside) Well really now
I think that's very prettily imagined.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: My Eveline shall have her will in all things.
There is no spot where we have breathed together,
That is not rich with happy memories.
Come, then, some other room. And if one care
Have sullied this for thee, 'tis closed for ever.
For I must have the roses on those cheeks
Come mantling back, and see those joyous eyes
Sparkle as bright as ever. I return
Claimant for long arrears of happiness,
And will not be defrauded of an hour!
Nay, nay, no drooping! Nothing now but smiles.
sp.stage: (leads her off, door L. 2 E.–Lord Arden comes out from the closet, and Maud makes a sign to him, then takes the key out of the door and locks it on the outside –when the door is locked Arden sinks into a chair)

div.head: SCENE FIRST.—Garden of Sir Walter Amyott's House.
div.stage: Enter Maud and Page, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: I tell thee, Mistress Maud, there is a secret.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I tell thee, Master Page, thou art a fool.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Everything proves it—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: What, that thour't a fool?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Psha! No; but that some dear delicious secret
Is giving interest to these dull old walls.
Now Maud—dear Maud—by all the tender love
Of sweet confections I have vowed so oft—
By all the charms of buttery and larder,
That spread a halo of delight about thee—
I do conjure thee—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: What an imp it is!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Maud, gentle Maud! Think of this gloomy house,
That has grown gray with premature old age,
And I with it, for want of some excitement
To vary our monotonous propriety—
Think of its now holding a mystery
To stir its pulse up to a healthy beat,
And I forbid my share! There's not a page
From Durham to Land's End that would endure it!
Maud—pretty Maud!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Thou arrant little fool!
I tell thee there is naught.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Maud, have a care!
My office gives me opportunities
Beyond the common herd—I've seen too much!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Seen! What?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Ha! Thou dost start,—thou'rt pale, sweet Maud!
Seen what? Hist!—no one's by. Why has my lady
Forsworn her Bower Chamber?

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Why? Because
She's weary of it. She has wept and watched there
So many hours, she made a solemn vow
That, should my lord come safely home at last,
No foot should enter it—no eye behold it—
'Till twelve months' joy had washed it sweet again.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Excellent Maud!—but lady most forsworn!
Forsworn, ere yet twelve hours of joy had passed!
Why did she—ah, she fancied, unobserved—
Steal, an hour back, in secret to that chamber?
Why did sweet Maud bear there a portly basket,
Hid in a napkin, and come empty forth?
Why lock the door so carefully behind her?

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Silence! The deuce is in the prying urchin!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Aha, good Maud!—is there no secret now?
There's more than that, though—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Peace, thou imp of mischief!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: There is a man concealed within that chamber.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Ha! Oh, thou wretch! Thou little slanderous viper!
I'll have thee hanged.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: I say, Maud, there's a man!
I heard his voice in converse with my lady,—
Heard him in talk with thee too. Maud, thou tremblest;
But do not fear—I am a gentleman.Treat me with confidence, I'll not betray you;
But, strive to hoodwink me and fool me, then
I'll be all spite and mischief—cross and vicious;
I'll go for counsel to old Jabez Sneed;I'll—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Curse upon the prying little vermin!
Well, since you know it then, there is a man.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Oho, my charming Maud!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: But, look you, sir,
There's life and death hang now on your discretion,
And if you speak—

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: My honour, Maud—my honour!
You have my honour.(laying his hand on his heart)But who is the man?
La! only think, my lady! Fie, fie, fie!
Oh, woman, woman, you're a feeble sex!
We men do play the very devil with
Your dear soft hearts! We ought to shew more mercy—
We really—(crosses L.)

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Why thou pestilent cock sparrow!
Is't possible thou dar'st with even a thought
Slander my precious lady! Know then, sir,
Since I must tell your prying pageship all things,
It is her brother.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Brother! Pooh, pooh, pooh!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I tell thee boy—I swear it—that the man
Hid in the Bower Chamber, is Lord Arden.
He has sought refuge here unknown to all,
'Till he can ship for France. And since, sir, now
You've got your will, and all that I can tell you,
I shall expect your aid.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: I'm disappointed.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: What!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Yes, I really am. A little mischief
Would have been quite refreshing to the dulness
Of this staid, pretty-mannered house.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Why now,
Thou little reprobate—

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Hush!—no ill language.
(whispers)Remember I'm a co-conspirator;
And, to offend me, may be dangerous.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Hist, for your life! We're watched!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Fear nothing, Maud;
Thou'rt under my protection.(leads her apart to L.)

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Whispering?Heads close together? Is he, too, made partner
Of the suspicions held against me? Fool
To give my papers up to her demand!
I deemed her asking them a woman's whim,
Which, if opposed, would but grow doubly strong;
Or, yielded to, would cease with its enjoyment.
But she has pried too curiously, and now
Locks up the chamber where they are bestowed;
Belike that they may testify against me.
Could I obtain them some few figures changed,
And all were well again; but, till that's done,
I lie within her malice. Mistress Maud!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Save thee, good master Jabez!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: My occasions
Require admittance to the Bower Chamber.

sp.speaker: Maud
sp.l: In verity, 'tis closed with a fastening,
Carnally called a lock; and Lady Eveline
Hath got the key. No one may enter there.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: But there be papers which Sir Walter needs,
And I must render him. Obtain the key
But for a minute, and the act shall be
Esteemed a goodly service.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Peradventure,
They be the papers which my lady wishes
Herself to render to Sir Walter's hand.
Those where she deem'd she found—ahem! thou knowest!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: It ill beseemeth servants and handmaidens
To strive against each other, when their profit
Is better found in goodly fellowship.
Cast in thy lot with mine; it may be, else,
That I may stir some matters, Mistress Maud,
In thine own office, that but ill become thee.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: In mine? Man, I defy you!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Even in thine.
There's pilfering in the larder! Choice provisions,
That neither reach master's nor servants' tables,
Vanish from out the house!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: There must be rats.(aside)Lord Arden has a monstrous appetite.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I am not given to care for creature comforts;
But I had put aside a little pasty
For my own use—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Last night his lordship's supper.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: 'Tis stolen!—gone!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: The pasty? Oh, the pasty—
Yes, the poor dog, old Rover—Rover ate it.
I would have beat him, but he seemed to say
'Twas only a small love of creature comforts
The steward's room had taught him.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: And the wine—
A flask of Burgundy that stood beside it,
Drank the dog that, too?

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Plague upon the wine!
Who shall have taken that?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: She's breaking down;
I must go in and rescue her.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: The wine!
The wine, I say!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Oh, fie, fie, Master Sneed!
You know I saw you drinking that yourself.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Me! Saw me drink it?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Thee—thee—Jabez Sneed.
The fumes of it are still upon thy breath.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Oh, quite unpleasant!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Scum! Do you dare to flaunt me?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Oh, it's rising!
It's getting in his head.(whispers aloud)He's drunk!
This is a sight unfit for female eyes.
Come, Maud; I must preserve thy purity(crossing R., with Maud)
Leave him. Oh, fie!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Oh, fie, fie, Jabez Sneed!

div.stage: Exeunt Maud and Page, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: They dared not do this, if they felt not sureMy ruin was determined. They make haste
Already to the spoil, and glorify
Themselves in my backslidings. How to oppose them?
If I could only shake her credit with him;
Make him, but by a shade, distrustful of her—.
Find something I could threaten with. But no;
She is too subtle. The accursed papers!
'Tis all shut up in them. What if I move him
To ask, as for himself, the chamber key?
She'll scarce deny him. If she give it—good!
If not, in the refusal, there's suspicion,
Which, managed tenderly, might—Hush! he's here.(goes up, L.)

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: My home, my home! Oh, what a wordless joy
To be in thy familiar clasp again!
In absence, it seemed almost too much bliss
For hope to picture. My returning foot
Paused on the threshold, doubting still, lest change,
Like a spring frost, had nipt some bud away;
But not a leaf is wanting! All's the same—
Love, peace, and joy flutter in every breeze,
And my full heart, too small for its great wealth,
Flows over with its rapture.(Jabez advances, L.)Ha! good Jabez!
The same as ever—up with the first dawn?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: The master suffers when the servant sleeps—
And forty years of sleepless service, make
Watching grow second nature. I'm a fool though—
My zeal but breeds revilings for my payment:
Yet 'tis for you.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: And me it binds your friend.
Of all the heritage my father left me,
I thank him most for thee, my honest Jabez.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Ah, well, well, well!—truly thou art a kind
And easy master—it may be too easy;
Yet servants and handmaidens, dogs, and horses,
All joy to see thee back!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Maids, dogs, and horses,
And never name their mistress! Fie! The flash
Of happiness that kindled on her cheek
At my approach—oh, that had more of joy
Than all a world beside, combined, could offer.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Well, well, perhaps it had.(crosses, L.)

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Only perhaps?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: How should I know? I have some little skill
In servants, horses, dogs—but none in women,
I thank my fortune, and my single state.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou graceless infidel! Would'st thou be blind
And thank the lot that hid from thee the rose?
Deaf, and rejoice thou could'st not hear sweet music?
Fie! fie on thee! The tongue that rails at woman
Blasphemes creation in its brightest work.
A ribald jest upon her purity
Is flinging stain upon the fairest mirror,
Where heaven is glass'd below.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I thought 'twas only
The trick of raving boys, to call earth heaven,
And women angels.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Nay, that do not I;
I would not so mis-name my Eveline.She is a woman!
'Tis the idiot's cant
To seek for what's of earth, a higher name.
The smile that darts its sunlight to our souls;
The sigh that melts and purifies our hearts;
The trembling tear that owns and pleads for weakness;
The timid trust that leans upon our strength;
Gentleness that refines our rugged nature;
And constancy nor time nor change can move;
All these are woman's—woman's own! 'Twould wrong her
To separate her name from her best graces.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: More wrong to make an idol of frail clay,
And worship it.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I worship not—I love!
A nearer, dearer word; whose human fondness
Encloses in its warm and holy clasp
Affection, tenderness, esteem, devotion,
Whatever heaven has left its creatures free
Unblamed to consecrate to one another.(crosses, R.)

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: And confidence?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What question of it?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: None.She's fair to look on, and, I doubt not good,
In full proportion. If on your return
She locks her chamber up against all access,
Except her favourite maid's, it needfully
Argues no want of confidence. Belike
'Tis but caprice.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Hast thou occasion there?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: My lady had some papers there to view,
Which I require.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Then ask her for the key.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Not I, not I; I'm somewhat old to brook
Refusal calmly, and she would refuse me;
For she doth ever make her sport to chafe me:
I know not for what cause.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I fear me, Jabez,
Thou art too harsh and churlish with her youth.
Remember, she's thy mistress, and my wife.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I pray you ask my mistress, then –your wife—
To give to you the key of the Bower Chamber.
'Tis like there is no mystery, yet if
There, peradventure, should be, she'll scarce choose
Disclose it to a servant; but from you
I would not think she could desire concealment—
'Twould grieve my spirit if she should.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What folly
Works now in thy suspicious brain?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Speak lower.
She comes. I pray you walk now by my counsel.(turns away, and retires, L.)

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh, Walter, I have been in search of thee!
Why, what is this?—A cloud upon thy brow!
Has aught gone wrong? Yet, even if there has,
Couldst thou but know the pains we have bestowed,
To make all bright and fit to welcome thee,
Thou would'st not be too critical. I meant not
To trust thee from my side till I had been
Thy guide to every favourite spot—I made it
My joy to think how thou would'st view a thousand
Little surprises I'd prepared for thee!
There's not a flower but I have watched its growth,
And hoped it would not bloom till Walter came;
There's not a bird sat singing on a tree
But I have tried to lure it there again
To make its constant perch till thou should'st hear it;
There's not a sight thine eye has ever loved
But I have sought to multiply it round,
To make more beauty for thy coming home;
And I had meant to lead thee on through all,
And find in thy delight a sum of joy
To pay me all thine absence.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: So thou shalt,
My priceless treasure, my unequalled wife!
And in thy smile, and thy sweet voice's tone,
Shall all of pleasant sights and pleasant sounds,
Gather around till earth grows Eden!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Then
Why did I find thee with so grave a brow?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: A passing folly—'twould but vex thee!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter,
Thou did'st not take me for a summer wife
To share thy sunshine only! That dear title
More strongly yet claims to partake thy pains.
What was the cloud I met upon thy brow?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Well, then, they'd tell me,
I have not all thy heart – that thou hast from me
Reserves, concealments ––

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Who's the enemy
Of both our peace, would dare to whisper that?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Dost thou say guilty? Has that open heart
Its chambers, where I must not ask admission
,And where I may not enter? Has it thoughts
It would not place, as in my open hand,
Where I might scan them throughly?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Well thou know'st
It is a book whose every character
Thou hast read o'er and o'er—whose open leaves
Thou canst search through at will:—and if a page
There be I would turn over from thine eyes,
'Tis only that which bears some household names
I may not quite blot out –and thou, alas,
Hast little pleasure viewing.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ever most pure and good!
I would not have one shade of heart or soul,
Other than what it is.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: There's something further!
I have not studied in those eyes so long,
To miss their meaning. There's a something yet,
Struggling for utterance, although forbid—
What is it?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Such a folly that I shame
To give it speech; but, thou hast locked a chamber
And borne away the key. Would'st thou object
To trust it to thy husband?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Did the same
Who told thee to suspect me of concealments,
Suggest this proof?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou'rt right—it was the same—
No second tongue could ever so insult thee,
Save mine, which now—

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter—

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou shalt not speak—I will not hear thee'
It would degrade thee answering such a doubt.
If thou desire thy chamber's privacy,
Thou shalt not even seem to ask my licence—
No, not a word; I will not listen! This
Is all the poor return that I can make.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter! I never had athought or wish
Would blush before thine eye. Were it not so,
I could not look thus fearless in thy face,
And meet thy confidence.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I know it, dear one!
Forgive, forget my folly. We have wasted
Too thriftlessly a crowd of precious moments;
For every moment's precious when with thee.
Come, thou shalt lead me to each favourite haunt,
And teach me what remains of Paradise
Earth still can boast, when love sits smiling there.
Come, Eveline!Leads her off, R. 1. E.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Fool! Idiot! Ass! She winds him
As pliant as a silken thread about her,
And foils me still. Can nothing—ha! the window;
I never thought of that. It may be open,
And I may reach them so. I'll make the attempt.

div.head: SCENE SECOND.—The Bower Chamber (as in Scene Second of First Act)—Daylight.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: It's very entertaining; but I wonder
Wherein this differs from a prison! only
Excepting in the article of food,
Which really's very decent. Memorandum—
Always be good friends with the housekeeper;
My constant maxim. But a prisoner
I am, to all intents, locked in my cell—
Hem!(looking round)Something better, too, than bare stone walls;
Yet there's a plague even in that. I've heard
Of captives who have made themselves amusement
With toads and spiders. No such luck for me!I've searched through every corner for a spider;But meddling housemaids have not left me one!
sp.stage: The door, L. 2 E., is heard to unlock; it then opens—enter Maud with a basket, and carefully locks the door after her.
sp.l: Ha, my sweet Maud! Why, in this very instant,
I longed—I pined for thee!(taking her hand)

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: 'Tis like, your lordship
Felt carnal cravings of the inward man
For worldly things.(pointing to the basket—shebrings forward the table and spreads the cloth)

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: A truce with canting, Maud!
It is not fit for rosy lips like thine.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: In verity,
Unless we exercise the gifts we have,
Our graces will grow rusty.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Say you so?
There then!(kisses her)

sp.speaker: Maud
sp.l: Good gracious! La! My lord!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: You know,
Except we exercise our gifts, good Maud,
Our graces will grow rusty. Ha, ha, ha!
I have not had so sweet a kiss these six months.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Nor I.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: In sooth, good Maud, I'm almost crazed
For want of occupation. Even the pastime
A prisoner finds in peeping through the bars,
I am forbid.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Why, if you would not have
A musquetoon be levelled at your head,
I should not recommend it. For heaven's sake
Never approach the window.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: I regard it
At most respectful distance as I saw there
A troop of Ironsides! Pest on it, Maud!
Is there no cock-boat will put out for France?
Will the wind never change? It's like a woman,
Flitting round all the points, when you would fix it.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Ay, like your mistress.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Only constant when
It holds you fast, and you would give the world
To change it—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Like your wife—Exactly so!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: I've wandered round this chamber, till I've learnt
Each stitch in all the tapestry by heart—
I'll tell you to a pane, how many squares
Are in that casement. I have reckoned up
The boards that form the floor—there's forty-seven.
I had some thoughts of numbering the nails—
In short, I've nothing left, but making love,
And none to love but thee!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Yes, here's a rival!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Psha! The bright ruby of thy lips—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Is nothing to this full flask of burgundy.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Thy hand—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Not half as tempting as a pigeon's leg,
That's in this pie.

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Thy round and buxom form(putting his arm round her)

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Mere nothing to the crust(crosses, L.)

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: I swear to thee—

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Thou'rt very hungry, so sit down and feed!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Thou art a sage, and know'st the the heart of man—
I honour thee, sweet Maud! Here's to thy health!(sits. Jabez appears at window, and seeing them,starts)

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Ha!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: What's the matter?

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: I—I thought I saw
Something that window—(Lord Arden is going to the window, when Maud stops him)Are you mad, my lord?
Stand back!(goes and looks out)No; there seems nothing—it was fancy.
But, to make doubly sure, I'll go myself,
And see outside.(going, L. 2 E.)

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Maud—Maud! one moment.(kisses her)

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Fie, then!

sp.speaker: Lord A.
sp.l: Only to keep my graces from the rust—
Excellent, Maud! I drink thy health again.(snatching a glass from the table—Maud pops her head on, and Arden starts from table to run after her)

div.head: Scene First.–
div.stage: Enter 1st and 2nd Servants, armed with guns, R.

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: It's useless asking Master Sneed his meaning.
Our business is obedience.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Verily.
But evening's closing in, –the wind grows cold!
The outward man feels ill at ease–the nights
Are dark and moonless.

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: What for that cares Jabez?
Our orders are to watch the whole night through
That none may enter, nor yet quit the house.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Belike Sir Walter, as a man of war,
Delighteth in precaution; or dost think
He has intelligence of some foul plot
Of the malignants?

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: Like enough, since Jabez
Swore us to secrecy, and bade us let
Our watch be known to none–only if any
Shall make attempt to enter or go out
We are to seize him, and in privacy
Give Master Sneed advice.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Ay, thou shalt seize him,
And I'll give notice in the house.

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: Why, man, thou'rt not afeard?

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Verily no, good William;
I am provided well with carnal weapons ;–
My gun is doubly loaded–peradventure
'Twould do more service, though, upon occasion,
If I should add another charge? There's room.

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: Thou hadst better give thyself a charge of this.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: What is it?

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: Brandy.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: Thou'rt a goodly youth!
I will keep watch beside thee.

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: Nay, my post
Is in the young plantation, by the window
Of the Bower Chamber–thine the house's front.

sp.speaker: 1st Serv.
sp.l: 'Tis ill-devised. Alone, we shall grow heavy
And, peradventure, sleep–I'll stay with thee :
So can we hold some profitable converse,
Strengthening from time to time our natural man,
And keeping guard with better vigilance;
Else were we undeserving trust!

sp.speaker: 2nd Servant.
sp.l: This way then.
I own I've no dislike for company,
And here's enough for both !

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Vat a diable! De vind is change, and all is ready for de pauvre gentilhomme; but vere is de petit garçon to tell him so? I say him, "Ven you see de vind, you soon see me–so look out."

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.p: Very true, Captain. So, having seen the wind, as you would say–me voici!

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Dat is de good littel boy.

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Vell, vell, big man den–as you vas be some day, if you grows a good deal first–aha! Come, all is ready! Vill I go to de lady?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.p: You go to my lady, you sea-horse! I'd have you know, sir, none but gentlemen of condition approach her! The very sight of you would make her faint. Why you look like a Polar bear, and smell like a stock-fish–Pah!

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Very well, littel boy! (half aside) I vas put dat insult in de bill! And now dépêche toi, make haste, for I vas not like to be found here. Dey peutetre take me for a spy, and hang me–vich désagréable–I vas put dat peril in de bill, too!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.p: Why, upon my honour I think if old Jabez Sneed were to catch you, nothing would give him greater pleasure.

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Jabez Sneed! And who vas Jabez Sneed?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.p: Oh, only our old steward; who, I doubt not, would think the blood of a Frenchman a sacrifice of a sweet savour!

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Aha! I vas remember him–Jabez Sneed–I vas put Jabez Sneed down in de bill, too. Oh, for certain, Jabez Sneed–I must charge for all my dangers.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.p: Mercy on us! I shall ruin poor Lord Arden if I stay with this purse-sucker any longer. There, good fellow, keep thyself out of sight in yonder shrubbery, and I will go arrange all with my lady. I need not bid you lie close. Remember Jabez Sneed. Ha, ha!

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Ay, ay! I vas remember Jabez Sneed–no fear–I vas charge two louis more for Jabez Sneed. He vould like if he vas hang me. Aha! lucky I vas take de precaution to bring viz me some littel marchandize– mouchoirs–bijouterie–parfumerie–just in case dey vas catch me; den I vas pass for vat dey call pedlar; and my English so remarkable parfait, nobody vas never suspect me for nosing as a native.

div.head: SCENE SECOND.
div.stage: Enter Sir Walter and Jabez, L. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I will not hear you more. You make me blush
At the forbearance that has let you speak
So much already. What your mistress does
Asks not your license, nor will I permit
You call in question aught she wills.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Speak lower;
And keep your counsel! There is little credit
In being known for a convenient husband.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What's this, sir?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Nothing. Yet it is convenient
When wives would have concealments, and the husbands
Keep their eyes closely shut. Tush–tush, Sir Walter!
Frowns do not frighten me; I'm an old man,
Gray in your father's service.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: That you are so,
Must plead the pardon of my sin to hear thee.
I know thou lovest me, in thy surly fashion–
But love, in narrow minds grows jealousy;
And, restless as a chained and kenelled hound,
Bays against all approachers!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Better bay
At every passing sound than sleep in quiet
When thieves are at the door. Well, say I bark–
Will you not look what stirs me?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Man, I tell thee
He who would slander, even with a doubt,
The woman who has placed her trust in him,
Proclaims his heart unworthy from that hour
For love to shelter there. Oh! ere I doubted
The perfect truth of her I once had loved,
Though all creation, joined with my own sense,
Were ranged in the opposite, I'd cling to her,
Call my own sense, and all creation liar,
And rather rush to shipwreck in my trust,
Than safely swim on doubts to a cold shore
On which my sunken sun had ceased to shine.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Mad, mad! The woman hath thrown spells upon him.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Sir, you presume on the permitted license
Of your long service; and I have done wrong
Bearing your testy insolence as humour.
I should have checked it earlier, knowing well
You never loved your lady.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Said I ever
That I did love her? I'm no flatterer;
That which my heart conceives, my tongue must utter.
Her presence is a burthen on my conscience!
Ill fared it ever with the chosen people,
When they brought in strange women to their tents.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: This is past all conceiving. Whence has sprung
The boldness that dares speak thus of my wife?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I bade thee, Walter Amyott, use thine eyes.
Thou wilt not. I must tear them open then;
Shew thee this idol of thy trust–this woman
Of a strange people! Shew 'tis not for naught
She locks her chamber. She hath there concealed
That which destroys thy peace–thine honour.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Villain!
What ho, within! Who waits there?
Send your lady.
Ho, Eveline! (then to Jabez) I do but let thee live
To blast thee with the sight of that pure virtue
Thy poisoned tongue would foul–to see thee writhe
As under the severe, but holy eye
Of an accusing angel– Enter Lady Eveline, R. 1 E. Eveline!
What hast thou done to make this wretch thy foe?
Wherein provoked his malice? There is something;
Why was it hid from me? Speak, Eveline!
I'll not endure delay.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Nay, love, be calm!
I had forgotten it in thy return,
So let it rest; not make thine earliest welcome
Complaint of those thou hast trusted.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ha! I knew it.
What was it? Tell me!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: At some fitter time.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: There's none so fit as this. Go on! What was it?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Of far too little worth to move thee thus.
Servants in wealthy houses think, perhaps,
Such pilfering their prerogative.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: 'Twas there then?
What, thou hast been too curious?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Was I wrong?
I thought thy wife was but another self;
And, in thy lengthened absence, thus I asked–
Perhaps 'twas scarce a woman's province, yet
I asked for his accounts.

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: Ay!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Searching them,
With more of thought than one of greater skill
Might have bestowed, I found –or thought I found–

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: He robbed me? And you told him this?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I did.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: And not unlikely, threatened that I too
Should know it?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Could I less?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I see it all!
Hound!
sp.stage: (flinging Jabez violently off, then clasping Eveline in his arms)
sp.l: Eveline, my wronged, my precious wife!
Canst thou forgive me?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: What?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What! That my ear
Could dare take in a sound which breathed against thee.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh yes, and pardon him who spoke it, too.

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: But I will pay thee with yet dearer love,
If dearer love can be–cherish thee more,
If that a heart so fond yet more can cherish.
Oh, I am all unworthy of thee yet!
This baser metal needs refining more,
To mix with thy pure gold (seeing Jabez, R.) Ha, dost thou dare
Remain here still?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: My honesty is questioned.
I claim to have the accusation proved.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou shalt, be sure on't.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Now, upon the instant.
The proofs, so called, are in that lady's hands,
Locked in the Bower Chamber. We will go there
Without delay; she hath the key! (aside to Sir Walter) She's pale!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Not now–to-morrow.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Lady, I say now!
At once I'll come to controversy with thee.
My reputation's touched, and cries aloud
For instant justice–instant!––

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: He is right.
If he has power to prove his innocence,
It cannot be too soon. Give me the key!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: No, Walter, no,–not now!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Tush! this is folly.
When 'twas but thy caprice I yielded to it,
And asked no questions–but now, weightier motives
Forbid that a mere whim should push back justice.
What! (with surprise) Silent still? Give me the key.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I will not.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.stage: (aside to Sir Walter, who stands amazed)
sp.l: You mark?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I might say, cannot–but I then
Should speak untruth. Do not be angry, Walter:
I'll tell thee one day, and thou'lt say I did
Rightly and well. Thou art not angry?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: No.
There, leave me for awhile. I would be private.
I have some further business with this man.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: You'll kiss me?–or I'll think you're angry.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: No,
Not now. A kiss should be the meeting springs
Of love's unruffled waters and just now,
There is a something stirring at my heart,
Disturbs its current.
Give it time to rest.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I will not vex you with more disobedience.
'Tis almost over! But a few short hours–
Yet, oh, that they were past! – and I once more
Sheltered no unshared thought. Only to-night!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.stage: (standing as if lost, and passing his hand over his brow)
sp.l: Down, wicked thoughts! I know she's good and pure–
Wise, noble, virtuous–who dares bid me doubt it?
I looked in her clear eyes, and they were steadfast,
And full of holy purpose. Though her cheek
Was pale, there flickered there no shade of shame.
'Twas open all. If such consist with evil,
Then devils people heaven, and all's a wreck !
Creation, shattered to its elements,
Mocks at all form, and maddens in confusion!
What, thou again! I'll speak with thee hereafter.
Thou heard'st she said to morrow. Come to-morrow;
But shun my sight till then; I'll do my best
To think no evil in the interval.
Begone! Yet stop. A seal upon thy tongue!
If thou shalt breathe in any living ear,
Or hint a thought, such as thou hast spoke in mine,
I'll tear the heart out of thy felon breast,
And fling it to my dogs! Beware!

div.stage: Enter 1st Servant, L. 1 E., whispers Jabez–then retires up, L.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Aha!
Sir Walter Amyot–my unthanked zeal
Had set a band of watchers round your house;
They have surprised a man in the plantation
That screens the window of the Bower Chamber;
Wilt please you to examine him yourself?
Or leave him to your servants' questioning?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Hellhound! Thou'rt on the track, then! Be it so!
Lead on, lead on!
And let me know the worst!

div.stage: Exit, followed by Jabez and 1st Servant, L. 1 E.

div.head: SCENE THIRD.
div.stage: Outside of Sir Walter's House–Night-lights down–the window of one of the rooms showing among the trees, L. C.
Enter Page L. 3 E.; Jabez follows cautiously.

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Where's my brave Captain gone? Lord Arden's ready,
And only waits my lady's last farewell–
Mercy! who's coming?

div.stage: Enter Sir Walter and 1st and 3rd Servants.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Lo! Behold, Sir Walter!
Here's this incarnate mischief on the watch!
What dost thou here?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: I–I–I–I'm in love,
And walk by moonlight.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: A transparent lie!
There is no moon!

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: I'm waiting for it!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Peace!
Boy, do not stir–move not a foot. And you–
Put on your hounds upon the scent.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.stage: (up c., having motioned to the Servants who go off into the plantation, R. 3 E.)
sp.l: Sir Walter–

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Silence! My ear can bear no sound–thy voice
The least of all. The night owl or the raven,
Were as sweet music to it! Enter Servants with Captain Baroque, R. 3 E. Come, what find you?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Oh, my poor Captain!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: This way; Drag him on!
Now spoke I truth? Look here!
sp.stage: (then looking at the Captain, pauses astonished)

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.stage: (crosses c.–after a moment's hesitation, turning and fixing his eyes on Baroque–starts)
sp.l: Why, what is this!
What scarecrow from the fields have you brought hither?
Speak, man, what art thou ?

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Vat you mean sare–scarecrow? I am von honest littel tradesman, you call pedlar, vid pretty trinkets. Ah, see! Vill you buy?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What unimagined foolery is here?
You, sir!
sp.stage: (seizing Jabez by the arm, and dragging him apart)
sp.l: Is this what you have sought to fright me with?
This bale of dirt–this bundle of gross foulness!
Is this the–the–Ha! ha! (laughing convulsively) I could go mad
With very laughter! Speak! Is this the plaything
You would accuse my wife of toying with?
Speak, wretch!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: My marvel is no less than thine.
I do confess I know this fellow not–
Belike, some thief––

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: Tief! Tief yourself, old man! I fancy tief take tings out of de house, not bring dem into it–fine tings like dese. (points to his box)

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Answer me, fellow–what is't brings thee here,
Prowling about my house at night?

sp.speaker: Captain B.
sp.p: I begs your excellence vas pardon me; but I vas told come very privately–de gentleman vat vant me–Jabez Sneed–vant buy some pretty tings for pretty housemaid, and nobody to know–aha!
sp.stage: (PAGE and SERVANTS burst into shouts of laughter)

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Thou man of lies–

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Oh, Master Sneed a housemaid !

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Sir Walter–

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Silence, sir! This fellow's story
May ask more search. Take him within the house,
I'll question him at leisure–and till then
Let none have speech of him. Stay!
You have watched;
There's for your pains.
And do not think it strange–
They told me thieves were out–'twas a mistake.
Give it no further tattling–go!
sp.stage: Exeunt Servants and Captain Baroque, L. U. E.–Jabez crosses to R.–Page lingers.
sp.l: Now, sirrah!
Tell me, shall I most think thee fool or villain?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Sir Walter, who this knave may be, 'tis true
I know not–but to this I will be sworn,
I saw a man–a man of courtly seeming–
By every outward mark, a gentleman,
In the Bower Chamber. (pointing to the window) There, this very day,
While it was barred to you.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What? Wilt thou dare
Still to persist––

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Ha! ha! ha! ha! ha!
Why, is it possible? Why, Master Sneed!
Did not you know me?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thee!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: A lie again!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Peace! Interrupt him not.
Boy, tell me all;
Confirm this, and I'll heap upon thee favours
Past all thy youthful fancy ever dreamed.
'Twas thou?

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: A foolish fancy, sir–to see
How I should show as a smart cavalier.
I donned a flowing wig and a plumed hat,
And strutted some five minutes to a mirror
With great complacency–

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Did'st see his face?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I will not lie! His back was turned to me;
But 'twas a man, and that I'll die upon!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou did'st not see him? 'Tis all clear as day;
And I am made the fool of thy perversions.
Away! ere I forget my knighthood and thy years,
And do thine age some violence. (crosses, R.) She's pure!
Spotless as new–born infancy; and I
Only less vile to listen to thy slanders
Than thou to utter them. That chamber–

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.stage: (laying his hand on Sir Walter's arm and pointing to the window, upon which the rising moon begins to shine)
sp.l: Hist!
sp.stage: (a figure appears in the room indistinctly)

sp.speaker: Page.
sp.l: Confusion! I must warn them.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Stay! we'll have
No masquerading more. Methinks, Sir Walter,
The room's not quite so desolate.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Well–well?
She said to-morrow. What more natural
Than that to-night–she should–
sp.stage: (the figure of Lady Eveline appears in the moonlight near the window)

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Look!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Yes; well?
I said so. What's in that? It is herself!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.stage: (to Page who struggles to get away)
sp.l: Stand still, boy–or I'll put my dagger in you!
sp.stage: (Lord Arden is seen to join her)
sp.l: See there–aha! Now–now spoke the dog truth!
Look here, sir. (dragging the Page forward) Here's the other–here he stands–
The pleasant cavalier!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: 'Tis a mad dream!
My overwrought, distempered brain breeds shadows!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: To both of us alike then. Dream you not
She locks his hands in hers?
Do you not dream––
Look! look! she––
sp.stage: (Lady Eveline is seen to throw herself on her brother's neck)

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ah!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Alas, poor gentleman!

div.head: Scene First.–
div.stage: Gallery in Sir Walter's House; door in the remote flat, and arms suspended against the wall; table, L. C.; chairs.
Sir Walter discovered seated in a chair, L., his face buried in his hands, Jabez standing beside him, R. of table.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Weak, weak! The flesh is weak, and it rebels
Despite the spirit–yet it must be done.
Ho! Walter Amyott!
Up and arouse thee! Sink not feebly thus,
Like a weak worldling. Up! thou hast surprised
The wicked in their tents! Up, and be doing!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What?

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Justice!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: What is that? – There is no justice!
I lavished on her love, whose liberal bounty,
Had it been lord of worlds, would have cast worlds
Before her feet, and felt a beggar still,
Having no more to offer. I gave to her
My heart and all its thoughts. I kept back nothing;
And, lest my soul with too absorbed devotion
Should worship its great wealth, I day and night
Gave thanks to heaven that lent me such a treasure,
And prayed to be more worthy its possession.
What have I done? What have I left undone,
That I should meet with such return? Out, out!
There is no justice!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: But there is revenge!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: No, not for me; the bruised worm may turn;
The crushed one suffers motionless.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Is't thus
A strong man and a soldier suffers ?

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: Strong?
Ay, there has been when, as the hot blood coursed,
I've felt as though this hand could rend an oak.
Look at it now! Were I to grasp a sword
An infant would disarm me–trembling fool!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: It shall not need the warrior's sword to punish.
A word will do it. Thou shalt yield him up
To the avengers of the righteous blood
That still cries out. Their banner is abroad,
And they shall recompense him.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: How! Betray
My personal enemy to party foes!
Bid public justice move for private hate!
Play traitor to a helpless wretch, whom still
My own hand dare not strike! ––Away with thee!
Ere sink so abject in my own esteem,
I'd be myself his guide to where, alone,
We might stand hand to hand, and foot to foot,
With none but heaven for umpire! (suddenly) But thou'rt certain
He has not fled already for thy life
Make me assured he's not escaped!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: He cannot;
I've set a watch again without the house,
And brought thee here within. He is still locked
In the Bower Chamber.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ay, but then the boy?
Ere this he has told her all, and she is warned.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: I heeded too for that–the boy is safe
In close confinement.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou'rt the pearl of watchdogs!
I love thee! But do all things quietly.
To trap is ever better than to hunt,
When 'tis unlawful game we follow.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Not a tongue can breathe to her
Her crime's discovery; she sits unconscious,
As doth the bird before the fowler's gun.
Not even the capture of that evil man
Of a strange land hath been revealed to her.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Eveline! Eveline! My wife! my wife!
Oh why did'st thou not die? I would have wept thee
With holy tears–embalmed thee in my heart–
Borne thy sweet image to my early grave,
And triumphed to rejoin thee! Eveline!
This is not true! 'tis some wild hellish plot,
By demons gendered! Thou–(to Jabez)–none likelier.
She said thou wast a knave. Say thou hast done this–
Confess it, and thy knavery shall be
Above all virtue! Dost thou crave for gold?
Take it! Take all I have! I prize it not.
Take all my wealth, my house, my honours, lands;
Strip bare my body–take my health–my strength–
My blood–my life! but spare me Eveline !

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Methought thine eyes bore evidence against her.
Bid them deny their knowledge.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: They are liars!
My sight–my senses–thou–myself–all false;
And only she is true! Oh, she was ever
Holy as Eve, ere Eve had forfeit Eden!
Pure as an infant's dreams! Go, send her to me;
None but herself shall tell me I have lost her.

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: And thou wilt hear–

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Not thee! Send me my wife!
And for thy soul still keep thy secret close.
I would not–it may be we yet mistake her–
Perhaps–perhaps she still is innocent–
She may–(impatiently) Go, go!
Oh, I am very, very miserable!
sp.stage: (buries his face in his hands: then suddenly starting)
sp.l: And when she comes, what shall I say? Entice her
To lie against my sense? To disavow
That which my eyes have seen? She dare not do it!
And yet the thought, to raise
On that soft childlike cheek the burning blush
Of crimson shame! Make those calm gentle eyes
Start wide with horror! Give my gross surmise
Language and words! Oh, would that I were dead!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ay.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: You sent for me.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I did.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: What is the matter?
sp.stage: (takes his hand affectionately–he draws it away with a shudder)
sp.l: How!
Reject my hand!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: It chilled me. There's a something in the touch
That mine revolts at. I have been but ill.
The air has been alive–dark faces glaring.
Cold clammy hands been laid on mine–I felt them
And tried to push them off but could not.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: We're alone––
Is there not something thou would'st say to me?
That heart is doubly sick
Which hoards its malady close shut within it,
As thine has done. Give it relief. Speak!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: What?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: That hidden thing which presses on thy soul,
A hideous nightmare–that which turns the smile
Upon thy lips, to poison in thy heart–
Which makes the light of day a sickening terror
Worse than night's gloom–till horrid night comes peopled
With prodigies that shriek again for day.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh, Walter! what is this?
Some sudden frenzy has possess'd his brain–
Without there!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Silence, madam! Rest you still.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Then look on me as thou art wont to look,
And let me soothe thy care! What is it, Walter?
I have performed ere now the nurse's office,
And, thou did'st tell me, skilfully. Rest now,
Thy head upon my heart.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Is it so large,
There still is room? How many will it hold?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: In such a sense as it is due to all,
Then all the human kind: but in that straiter
And inner clasp where love lies hoarded up,
Then only thee–and well thou knowest it.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Wondrous?
This is past credit! I'm my senses' fool.
My eyes grow dim, and lack the power to see.
No blush! no mark of shame! her open brow
Raised boldly still! Oh, monstrous prodigy!
Hast thou no sense? Dost hear, see, understand,
And yet not tremble ?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Wherefore should I, Walter?
Whate'er the passion that distracts thee, well
I know thou would'st not harm thine Eveline,
Who never knew a word, a thought, a look,.
That was not love for thee.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: 'Tis past my skill!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Lean on thy wife–thy throbbing head will find
No resting place so true–

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.stage: (stares at her with blank astonishment–then, passionately)
sp.l: I'll hear no more!
My reeling brain grows mad! Speak, Eveline !
Does that fair seeming form shut in an art
Beyond a devil's skill, or art thou yet
That which thou look'st to me? Hast thou indeed
Betrayed my boundless confidence? Brought in,
Even beneath my father's sacred roof,
The blaster of my honour? Eveline,
Speak! I will trust it from no lip but thine–
Answer me, Eveline!

sp.speaker: Jabez.
sp.l: Where is Sir Walter?
All is confusion in the court below.
A troop of horse is halted at the gate;
And he who bears command enquires for you.
He says that a malignant has been tracked
To shelter here.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Aha! They answer for thee!
Hear you this, madam? What, you bend at last?
You are pale–you tremble now for him!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: For him?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Thou thought'st the searching, universal eye
That spies through every corner of the land,
Hoodwinked as easy as a weak, fond husband's?
That none would whisper a young cavalier,
Crept into Amyott's house–was welcomed there–
Locked in the lady's chamber! Ha, ha, ha!
Oh, it was nothing! Such a thing of course
Could wake no comment

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: He knows all. Oh, Walter,
Thou wilt not yield him to his enemies?
Thou wilt forgive him–shield him!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Mad! She's mad!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Shall I be blamed that he is dear to me?
Oh, could'st thou tell how dear! True he has wronged thee,
Yet never shews a noble mind so great
As when it pardons.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Viper!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter! Husband!
What would'st thou do?

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: His blood! Stand off! His blood!
My very soul's athirst for it. Beware!
Thine own life is not safe.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Why take it then,
So his be spared! Living thou shalt not pass me!
Here is my post, and if thou wilt advance,
My heart lies first upon thy bloody road.
sp.stage: (Sir Walter pauses, staring on her with amazement)

sp.speaker: Sir W.
sp.l: Stand quickly from my path
Or take the consequence–(approaching her)

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: Sir! Are you mad?
What would you do–frighting your helpless wife?
Turn back your passion on its proper road–
There's food enough for it. Your rank is outraged;
Your station scorned. A saucy Jack below
Would search your house like any common tavern,
And force his way inside.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Give him admission;
'Tis well! A private death were too much mercy.
A public shame; do you hear, madam? public!
Give the man entrance.

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: You'll not suffer this?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Out, useful go-between! Who waits there? Bid them
Give free admittance to whoever asks it.
Throw wide the doors–we've no concealments here;
Oh, no concealments–none!

sp.speaker: Maud.
sp.l: My precious lady,
All's lost–all's lost! Soldiers surround the house.
Six mounted troopers watch beneath his window!
What's to be done? What's left for us?

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Despair!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Well, sir, you have some business here?

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: The Lord Protector deigns to honour me–
Albeit but a weak, unworthy vessel–
With his commission. I have learned a traitor,
A fugitive malignant, here hath sheltered.
I come to seize him.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Ask the lady yonder,
My wife–I am myself but new come home,
And, in my absence, she has kept the house;
Ask her whom she has entertained.

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: Ha, ha!
There is not many a pretty wife will tell
Whom she has feasted in her husband's absence.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Wretch!
But no–you
Your answer, madam?

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: Speak!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: That I, in England, am the only lady
Whose husband would sit calmly by her side
And hear her thus insulted.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Still so bold!

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: Come, my fair mistress; bring the gallant forth,
Or I shall drag him out. There's not a corner
In all your house, wherein the rat can hide;
But I'll put in a ferret there.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Proceed, sir!
The lady, she will bear you company–
Shew you the way–point out her secret chambers.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: I'll not believe this.
You cannot be so changed. Deny it, Walter!
Deny this semblance of your outward person,
That bears no vestige of your better mind.
Have mercy, Walter!
On me, for my sake, for those happy years
Of never-clouded love that we have known;
If for no present love, still–still for these,
Be merciful!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: I have mistook my strength–
I cannot do it.
The blossom I have nursed upon my breast,
Though stained and soiled, I cannot fling it out
For common feet to trample on.

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: Time wastes.
Come, my fair lady, do you give us entrance,
Or shall I call my men and force it?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Neither, sir!
This house is mine, and I forbid you place
Further a foot within it.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Walter! Husband!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: My name, my services, are known enough
To place me above taint of all suspicion;
And, if not so, then be the risk my own
To say you shall not set another foot,
Nor further glance an eye within my walls.

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: Hereon I do attach you as a rebel;
And for the rest, I've force enough without
To level your foundations.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: At your peril!
For any drop of blood that's shed this day,
Account shall be demanded of you fourfold.
Begone!

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: By my good troth, and who is this
That talks so big?

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: It seems you know not, sir;
'Tis fit you learn then. I too hold commission
From the same source as you, but so above you,
That I have power, if I will, to punish
Your disobedience as rebellion. Here,
Within this district, all men bearing arms
Are under my command.

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: You will forgive me
If of such sudden news I ask for proof.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Read it, sir!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: Oh, my noble, generous husband!
Turn not so coldly from me still!

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Well? Now, sir!
Are you content?

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: I see 'tis worded so,
And must submit. But look, Sir Walter Amyott,
To have these things reported to his Highness.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: At your discretion. But report as well
The insolence that galled me. Tell him, then,
That sovereign rulers should shew better care
To whom they delegate authority.
That the most sacred power, wielded by hands
Of low adventurers and greedy ruffians,
Becomes a thing abhorred. Go! Get you gone, sir!
And seek another district. You shall make
No longer stay in mine!

sp.speaker: Officer.
sp.l: You shall hear further
Of this, be certain.
Nor will I be foiled
So lightly of my hope. I'll linger near,
And have him yet.
sp.stage: Exit, L. 1 E., leaving the door open.

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.stage: (flinging herself at Sir Walter's feet, and seizing his hand)
sp.l: Oh, Walter, my adored!
My worshipped husband! Thou dost love me still!
Strive not to draw that precious hand away:
I will not part with it! Oh, could'st thou guess
The flood of unimagined happiness
That drowns my sense to know thou still dost love me,
Thou would'st not strive to wear so cold a brow!
Though thou should'st spurn me now, I'd yield no jot
Of that blest faith. Nay, though thine angry hand
Should even strike thy dagger in my breast,
Still, still I'd swear thou lov'st me.

sp.speaker: Sir Wal.
sp.l: Be content–
I have shielded thee!

sp.speaker: Lady E.
sp.l: At peril of thyself.
